title: Literal boolean values should be avoided in assertions
description: Literal boolean values should not be used in assertionsAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmelltestsWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoUsing literal boolean values in assertions can lead to less readable and less informative unit tests. When a test fails, it’s important to have a clear understanding of what the test was checking and why it failed. Most of the testing frameworks provide more explicit assertion methods that will provide a more helpful error message if the test fails.ExceptionsIn the context of xUnit,Assert.TrueandAssert.Falseare not flagged by this rule.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
// Noncompliant: Using literal boolean in assertion
Assert.AreEqual(true, result); // Noncompliant

// Noncompliant: Comparing with boolean literal
Assert.AreEqual(false, IsValid()); // Noncompliant
compliant:
// Compliant: Using specific assertion methods
Assert.IsTrue(result); // Compliant

logic.reasoning: Detects Assert.AreEqual() calls with boolean literals (true/false) as first parameter. These should use Assert.IsTrue/IsFalse for better readability and error messages. Uses custom function to handle multi-line assertions.
